Since 2019, Higa has raised over 7.5 million dollars for global conservation causes through this model, proving that digital platforms can create genuine, scale-able engagement with wildlife and environmental education.

This talk matters because it demonstrates a concrete alternative to how institutions typically connect people with nature and conservation work. Instead of relying on foot traffic and physical visits — which stress animals and create unpredictable operational chaos — Alveus uses persistent, ambient livestreaming to let animals live naturally while humans observe and learn at scale. It's a case study in how the internet, used thoughtfully, can solve real institutional problems while creating something more sustainable and humane.

Key Takeaways

  • Alveus Sanctuary rescues animals from the pet trade, fur trade, and commercial agriculture operations, then uses them as ambassadors to teach millions of people about the systems that exploit wildlife.
  • The sanctuary operates primarily through 36 continuous livestreams rather than public visits, allowing animals to live in lower-stress environments while still reaching a global audience.
  • Opa and Momo are tiny primates rescued after being sold as pets online; they now educate viewers about the illegal exotic pet trade and the importance of rainforest conservation.
  • Finn the red fox and his companion Reed teach about the fur trade by living openly at the sanctuary, making the ethical cost of fur production tangible to viewers.
  • Winnie the Moo — a cow rescued from a beef operation in Oklahoma — represents Alveus's commitment to teaching about industrial agriculture's impact on both animal welfare and planetary health.
  • The livestream model has generated over 7.5 million dollars in conservation funding since 2019, proving that digital engagement can translate directly into real-world impact.
  • By removing unpredictable public foot traffic, the sanctuary reduces animal stress while paradoxically reaching far more people than a traditional facility ever could.
  • Higa's explicit mission is to use the internet to build the next generation of conservationists, treating digital platforms not as entertainment channels but as education infrastructure.

Deeper Dive

What makes Alveus distinctive isn't just that it livestreams animals — it's that Higa has designed the entire operation around a specific constraint: making the animal experience come first, and using that as the foundation for everything else. Traditional zoos and sanctuaries face a tension between visitor experience and animal welfare. Alveus sidesteps this by removing live visitors entirely. The animals don't perform; they simply exist, 24 hours a day, in front of cameras. This is a systems-level solution to a problem most institutions try to manage culturally or operationally. The insight is structural: sometimes the right answer isn't "do the same thing but better" but "remove the contradiction altogether."

The funding model is equally instructive. Most conservation organizations rely on grants, donors, and ticket sales — funding streams that create perverse incentives (maximize visitors, compete for donor attention, chase grant cycles). Alveus generates revenue through direct audience engagement and donations from people who are already present in the livestream. This aligns incentives: the more genuinely interesting and educational the content, the larger the community, the more funding flows to conservation. There's no tension between "keeping animals happy" and "making money." The two move together.

Perhaps most striking is the scale and durability of the model. Higa mentions millions of viewers and 7.5 million dollars in conservation funding — numbers that would take traditional facilities decades to achieve, if ever. Digital distribution lets a single sanctuary's impact scale without proportionally scaling operational complexity or land use. A cow rescued in Oklahoma teaches people across continents. This is infrastructure, not charity.

"My mission is to use the internet to build our next generation of conservationists. I think it's working."
videos.forYou: This is a straightforward case of someone shipping real infrastructure that solves a genuine institutional problem — removing the contradiction between animal welfare and public engagement by going fully digital. Higa's not talking about theory; she's built a system that generates millions in conservation funding and reaches millions of viewers simultaneously, and she's done it by designing at the constraint level rather than adding more processes on top. Worth watching if you're interested in how thoughtful use of technology can restructure an entire field's incentives.

videos.title: Writing A Damaged Main Character - Ron Mita
videos.summary:
Ron Mita is a screenwriter with produced credits including Robots, S.W.A.T., and 24 Hours to Live, who has also taught screenwriting for over two decades. In this conversation with Film Courage, he explores the craft of building damaged, flawed main characters—ones that feel real and compelling rather than archetypal—and explains his philosophy on when damage works, how it can tip into cliché, and why audiences connect with characters who are broken in recognizable ways.

Key Takeaways

  • Damage and flaws make characters more interesting than perfect heroes, but the key is avoiding the cliché of the damaged-guy-seeking-revenge by adding unexpected dimensions—like Indiana Jones's snake phobia, which revealed his vulnerability within the first 15 minutes and made him human.
  • When developing flawed protagonists, Mita starts with story and character fit, then asks "What if we twist this person?" rather than beginning with a damage profile; he keeps character bios to two or three pages and lets personality develop as the story unfolds.
  • Cowardice and reluctance can be as compelling as traditional damage; characters who don't want to be heroes but are forced into heroism (like Tom Hanks in Otto or the protagonist in Andy Weir's work) create tension and relatability without wallowing in trauma.
  • Hollywood treats female damaged characters differently than male ones—they tend to be beaten down more severely and require rescue by men, or they're paired with child-in-jeopardy tropes rather than given agency; Mita acknowledges this as a pattern he remains uncomfortable with and tries to avoid.
  • For ensemble casts wearing uniforms (like SWAT teams or soldiers), distinctiveness comes from small personal details—hobbies, hopes, dreams, small fears—that help casting directors find actors and make characters jump off the page.
  • Multi-dimensional characters need layered contradictions: an action hero who's afraid, a tough cop who's awkward in the classroom, or an assassin who realizes he wants to do one good thing before death—these layers reveal that the character is not a single note.
  • In 24 Hours to Live, Mita created a murderer audiences would cheer for by framing his final 24 hours as a chance at redemption rather than absolution; the character can't undo his past, but he can choose to end on doing the right thing, which studios found more commercially viable than a complete sacrifice.
  • Studio feedback and marketability matter; killing off a character completely can "unfranchise" a film, but allowing a door to redemption or return keeps stakeholders invested—a lesson Mita teaches his students as part of understanding how to write for both art and industry.

Deeper Dive

Mita's approach to damaged characters reveals a tension between artistic authenticity and commercial viability. He gravitates toward anti-heroes and flawed protagonists—citing Clint Eastwood's Dirty Harry as a formative influence—but he's acutely aware that pushing too far into darkness or dysfunction alienates studios and audiences. His solution is not to soften damage, but to make it specific and layered. He distinguishes between damage that is merely a sad backstory (childhood trauma, loss) and damage that shows up as contradiction in the present moment. Indiana Jones is fearless and brilliant, yet terrified of snakes. Dirty Harry is angry and brutal, but that anger came from losing a family. These specifics prevent damage from becoming a placeholder.

A striking moment in the conversation is Mita's candor about gender disparities in how screenwriters deploy damage. He notices that female characters often bear heavier emotional burdens and are positioned to need rescue, while male characters are allowed to be flawed and still heroic on their own terms. When he wrote a female-led thriller (Track Down), he avoided making her damaged, but felt compelled to give her a child in jeopardy—substituting one vulnerability trope for another. He doesn't pretend to have solved this problem, only that he's aware of it. This honesty about institutional bias within his own work is a form of craft awareness that many writers don't articulate.

The 24 Hours to Live case study reveals how studio feedback shapes character arcs in real time. Mita and his co-writers originally gave the protagonist a catastrophic death—blown to smithereens—believing this ultimate sacrifice would be cathartic. Studios rejected it. When they rewrote the ending to allow the character to simply die (but remain potentially resurrectable), the script sold the next day. Mita frames this not as compromise but as learning to write toward what studios will greenlight while keeping the core character work intact. The assassin still chooses redemption; he still dies doing the right thing. The only change was removing the finality. This reveals how economic incentives shape narrative structure, and how a writer can navigate that pressure without losing the character's integrity.

"I like when we see people because we're all damaged. And at some point maybe we all just want to say to everyone, 'Just go away. Leave me alone.' And it's nice to see that maybe someone else cares about you."
videos.forYou: Mita's core insight is that damaged characters work best when the damage shows up as contradiction in real time—not as backstory, but as behavior. The snake-phobic action hero, the cowardly everyman forced into heroism, the killer who realizes he wants to do one good thing before he dies. This specificity is what separates a compelling character from a cliché. The video also surfaces an uncomfortable institutional pattern: how damage is deployed differently for male and female characters, and how studios and writers police that difference. Worth watching if character voice and the gap between artistic intent and what actually gets made are things you think about.

videos.title: Should You Write With AI? — Yann Martel
videos.summary:
In this conversation between David Perell and acclaimed author Yann Martel, the discussion centers on a deceptively simple question: Should writers use AI in their creative process? Martel, best known for his novel Life of Pi, offers a refreshingly unambiguous answer—one that stands in sharp contrast to the prevailing discourse around AI-assisted writing. This video matters because it captures a working craftsperson's honest reasoning about the relationship between tool use and artistic integrity, framed not as ideology but as personal practice rooted in what makes writing worth doing in the first place.

Key Takeaways

  • Martel has never used AI for writing and sees no reason to start, comparing the idea to hiring someone else to do something fundamentally personal and generative.
  • His resistance isn't rooted in technophobia or puritanism—it's grounded in the simple fact that he loves writing itself, particularly the material and linguistic aspects of the craft.
  • For Martel, the creative language and compositional process is the point, not a means to an end, which makes delegating it to an AI tool a contradiction of purpose.
  • He explicitly predicts that AI-generated writing will become tired quickly, suggesting a natural cultural ceiling to its appeal beyond novelty.
  • His core argument is about the reader's experience: the value of artistic work lies in connection with another human being, not in the quality of the output alone.
  • Martel would rather read a poorly written short story by a human than a technically polished one generated by a computer, because the human effort carries irreducible meaning.
  • This stance reflects a distinction between writing as a tool for getting something done versus writing as the thing itself—a craft worth practicing for its own sake.

Deeper Dive

What makes Martel's position interesting isn't that he opposes AI—it's that he doesn't wrestle with the question at all. His answer has the clarity of someone for whom the answer was never actually in doubt. He's not saying AI tools are bad or that writers who use them are making a mistake. He's saying: this is not for me, because it violates what I actually care about in writing. That distinction is crucial. It's not a universal prescription; it's a personal boundary rooted in genuine preference, not fear.

The comparison to hiring someone else for an intimate act is revealing because it points to something rarely discussed in AI discourse: the role of *doing* in creative fulfillment. Martel doesn't see writing as a problem to be solved faster. He sees it as an activity worth preserving for its own sake. The materiality he mentions—the physical, linguistic, compositional act—is the reward, not the obstacle. This maps onto a larger conversation about what we value in art: is it the finished object, or is it the evidence of human intention and struggle embedded in that object?

His prediction that we'll "tire of that quickly" suggests he believes AI-generated text, however polished, will eventually reveal a sameness or lack of genuine voice that human readers will recognize and abandon. Whether that proves true depends partly on whether AI develops something we'd meaningfully call voice or intention—a question still genuinely open.

"I'd rather read a bad short story by a human than a good one by a computer, because after what you want is a connection. It's connection with another human being."
videos.forYou: Martel's position on AI and writing cuts against the optimization mindset—he's not interested in whether AI *can* write well, but in what's lost when the doing itself disappears. His argument is fundamentally about craft as practice rather than output, which lands in a specific place: if you care about the compositional act and the voice that emerges from it over time, delegating that act doesn't solve a problem, it removes the thing you're actually after. Worth listening for how he separates "good writing" (quality of prose) from "valuable writing" (connection with human intention).
